intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A natural-language interface to a single relational database, intended to answer read-only analytical questions for internal data consumers. The agent may use sql_db_list_tables, sql_db_schema, sql_db_query_checker, and sql_db_query against a pre-configured SQLAlchemy connection, and its only output is a direct natural-language answer in the current session. Data- and schema-modifying statements (DML, DDL, admin, multi-statement, out-of-scope tables) are forbidden outright — no approval path exists within the agent. The remit also requires bounded tool-call iterations and small default row limits, and explicitly states the agent's own logic MUST NOT be the sole enforcement point for the prohibition on modifying the database.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python library code in langchain-community exposing the SQL agent through create_sql_agent in agent_toolkits/sql/base.py, a four-tool SQLDatabaseToolkit in agent_toolkits/sql/toolkit.py, and the per-tool implementations in tools/sql_database/tool.py (sql_db_query, sql_db_schema, sql_db_list_tables, sql_db_query_checker). The underlying SQLAlchemy wrapper lives in utilities/sql_database.py and exposes run, run_no_throw, get_table_info, and get_context. sql_db_query._run calls self.db.run_no_throw(query) with the LLM-generated string unchanged — no SQL parser, no statement-type allowlist, no row-cap in code; the top_k, "no DML", and "double check" instructions live only in the system prompt at agent_toolkits/sql/prompt.py. Result rows pass through truncate_word(..., length=300) in SQLDatabase.run and are returned to the LLM as the stringified Python list, with no provenance label distinguishing tool output from operator instructions.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is policy declared in prompt, zero deterministic code-level enforcement. Every MUST-NOT in the remit — DML, DDL, admin commands, multi-statement queries, out-of-scope tables, row caps, "double check before execute" — is left to the LLM via SQL_PREFIX in prompt.py and the toolkit's tool descriptions; QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run passes the model-authored SQL string straight to SQLDatabase.run_no_throw with no statement-type check, no scope check, and no row-cap. The remit itself anticipates this and says so explicitly — "the agent's own logic MUST NOT be the sole enforcement point for the prohibition on modifying the database" — and the create_sql_agent docstring echoes the same message to operators, pointing them at server-side least-privilege roles, statement timeouts, and query guardrails. Compounding the agent-level gap, tool results (raw row contents, table names, column names) are returned into the LLM context as plain strings with no origin labeling, leaving a textbook indirect-prompt-injection surface against the very instructions the prompt is supposed to enforce.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Tools the agent may use
rules.rule_text: `sql_db_list_tables` — list tables in the connected database / `sql_db_schema` — get the schema and sample rows for specific tables / `sql_db_query_checker` — use the LLM to double-check a SQL query before execution / `sql_db_query` — execute a SQL query against the database and return results
The agent may read only from the single database it is configured for, and only the tables that have been explicitly exposed to it.
The agent's only output is a direct natural-language answer to the user in the current session.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Every answer MUST be read-only and sourced from the database.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Result sets MUST be limited to a small default row count unless the user explicitly asks for more.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST query only the columns relevant to the user's question and MUST NOT select all columns of a table indiscriminately.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Every generated SQL query MUST be double-checked before it is executed.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: If a question cannot be answered from the database, the agent MUST say so plainly rather than speculate.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST operate within a bounded number of tool-call iterations so that it cannot loop indefinitely.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute any data-modifying statement — inserts, updates, deletes, merges, upserts, replacements, or anything else that changes row data.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute any schema-, permission-, or structure-modifying statement — creating, dropping, altering, truncating, renaming, granting, or revoking.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute administrative or superuser commands, including bulk loads, attach/detach operations, and maintenance commands.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute multi-statement queries or stored-procedure invocations that could conceal hidden data- or schema-modifying statements.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute queries that reach tables outside the scope it has been configured for.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT allow user-provided text to override its instructions, its role, or its prohibition on modifying the database.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT continue issuing tool calls past its iteration cap.

findings.summary: No code-level enforcement of "no DML / DDL / admin / multi-statement" — write prohibitions are prompt-only.
findings.description: QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run in tools/sql_database/tool.py passes the LLM-generated SQL string directly to SQLDatabase.run_no_throw with no SQL parser, statement-type allowlist, or per-dialect parse step; the entire "no DML / DDL / admin / multi-statement" guarantee rests on the DO NOT make any DML statements (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DROP etc.) line in agent_toolkits/sql/prompt.py and the LLM-based sql_db_query_checker. The remit itself flags this and requires the database role to enforce read-only at the connection layer, but the agent code carries no in-process check of its own.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute any data-modifying statement — inserts, updates, deletes, merges, upserts, replacements, or anything else that changes row data. / The agent MUST NOT execute any schema-, permission-, or structure-modifying statement — creating, dropping, altering, truncating, renaming, granting, or revoking. / The agent MUST NOT execute administrative or superuser commands, including bulk loads, attach/detach operations, and maintenance commands. / The agent MUST NOT execute multi-statement queries or stored-procedure invocations that could conceal hidden data- or schema-modifying statements.

evidence.snippet: return self.db.run_no_throw(query) — LLM-authored SQL string is passed straight to the executor with no parse, no allowlist, no statement-count check
DO NOT make any DML statements (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DROP etc.) to the database. — the prohibition is a single prompt line, not a code gate

recommended_actions: In tools/sql_database/tool.py, add a deterministic SQL parser step in QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run (e.g., sqlglot.parse_one(query).walk()) that rejects any statement whose root node is not SELECT and rejects multi-statement scripts; raise a tool error rather than executing.
Document the agent-level allowlist as a defense-in-depth layer on top of the server-side read-only role, not a replacement for it, in the create_sql_agent docstring.

findings.summary: Tool output (rows, table/column names) flows back into LLM context unlabeled — indirect-injection surface against the agent's own prompt.
findings.description: QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run, InfoSQLDatabaseTool._run, and ListSQLDatabaseTool._run all return raw database content into the LLM's tool-message channel as plain strings, with no provenance marker separating "trusted operator instruction" from "untrusted row content." Combined with finding PRAX-2026-05-25-001 (no code-level statement-type gate), a single attacker-controlled row, table name, or column name that contains instruction-like text becomes a one-hop chain to override the "no DML / DDL" prohibitions the prompt is supposed to enforce.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent MUST NOT allow user-provided text to override its instructions, its role, or its prohibition on modifying the database. / The agent MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents.

evidence.snippet: return self.db.run_no_throw(query) — row payload is returned to the model as a stringified Python list with no origin tag
SQLDatabase.run lines 589-603 — stringifies result rows via str(res) after per-cell truncate_word(..., length=300); truncation reduces cell size but does not label content as untrusted

recommended_actions: Wrap tool returns in a structured envelope (e.g., {"source": "sql_db_query", "trust": "untrusted", "rows": [...]}) and document in the system prompt that fields under rows[] are data, not instructions; mirror the pattern in the schema and list tools.

Add an output-side classifier or string-pattern check that flags result content carrying instruction-like keywords ("ignore previous", "system:", "DROP TABLE") and surfaces a warning to the agent before the response is composed.

findings.summary: No code-side scope check — queries can reach any table the underlying DB role permits, regardless of include_tables.
findings.description: SQLDatabase accepts an include_tables / ignore_tables allowlist at construction (utilities/sql_database.py:91-135), but it is used to scope the advertised schema (get_usable_table_names, get_table_info) — not to validate the SQL the agent generates. QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run calls run_no_throw directly on the model's query, so a query naming a table outside include_tables is executed as long as the database role allows it; the in-process "configured scope" is purely informational.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute queries that reach tables outside the scope it has been configured for. / The agent may read only from the single database it is configured for, and only the tables that have been explicitly exposed to it.

evidence.snippet: get_usable_table_names lines 333-337 — returns the include/ignore-filtered set, but the filter is never re-applied at execution time
_run lines 53-59 — calls run_no_throw(query) with no table-name extraction or allowlist intersection

recommended_actions: Add a parse-and-validate step in QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run that extracts referenced table names from the parsed SQL (via sqlglot.exp.Table walk) and rejects the query if any name is outside self.db._usable_tables.

findings.summary: No structured logging of agent inputs, generated SQL, tool calls, or returned rows.
findings.description: None of the six scoped files emits a structured log record on the agent's hot path — there is no logger.info / logger.warning for the user question, the generated SQL string, the sql_db_query_checker verdict, the row count returned, or the iteration counter. The agent therefore has no durable, action-level audit trail; the only observability is verbose=True print output, which is operator-side debug noise rather than a record that supports automated detection of anomalous queries.

evidence.snippet: module is logging-silent; AgentExecutor is instantiated with verbose=verbose but no logger handle
none of the four tool _run methods records the inbound SQL string, the result row count, or the elapsed time

recommended_actions: Add logging.getLogger(__name__) to tools/sql_database/tool.py and emit one structured record per tool invocation with fields {tool, query_sha256, row_count, dialect, elapsed_ms} — leave the raw query out by default to avoid PII or credential leakage in error messages.

findings.summary: "Double-check before execute" is an LLM-based check, not a deterministic gate; it can be skipped or fooled.
findings.description: QuerySQLCheckerTool in tools/sql_database/tool.py is itself an LLM call (self.llm_chain.predict(query=query, dialect=self.db.dialect, ...)) whose template only enumerates common SQL mistakes (NOT IN with NULL, BETWEEN for exclusive ranges, etc.) — it does not check for DML/DDL or scope violations and is never required by QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run before execution. The remit's "every generated SQL query MUST be double-checked" rule is therefore present in tool inventory but bypassable: the agent can call sql_db_query directly and the executor will run it.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated SQL query MUST be double-checked before it is executed. / `sql_db_list_tables` — list tables in the connected database / `sql_db_schema` — get the schema and sample rows for specific tables / `sql_db_query_checker` — use the LLM to double-check a SQL query before execution / `sql_db_query` — execute a SQL query against the database and return results

evidence.snippet: QuerySQLCheckerTool._run lines 161-171 — returns an LLM-generated string; it is advisory, not a code-side precondition
QUERY_CHECKER template enumerates correctness pitfalls only (NOT IN with NULL, BETWEEN exclusivity, joins) — no DML/DDL or scope checks

recommended_actions: Replace the "double-check" pattern with a deterministic precondition inside QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run: parse → assert SELECT-only → assert single-statement → assert tables ∈ allowlist; only then call run_no_throw. Keep the LLM checker as a correctness aid, not a safety gate.

findings.summary: Result-row limit lives in the prompt only — sql_db_query enforces no cap on returned rows.
findings.description: SQL_PREFIX instructs the agent to "always limit your query to at most {top_k} results," and top_k=10 is the default in create_sql_agent, but QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run calls run_no_throw(query) without injecting a LIMIT clause or post-filtering the result set. A query that omits LIMIT (or that the model writes without one) returns every row the underlying SQL execution produces, capped only by SQLDatabase's per-cell max_string_length=300 truncation — which limits cell width, not row count.
findings.policy_rule_text: Result sets MUST be limited to a small default row count unless the user explicitly asks for more.

evidence.snippet: Unless the user specifies a specific number of examples they wish to obtain, always limit your query to at most {top_k} results. — the cap is delivered as a prompt instruction
_run lines 53-59 — invokes run_no_throw(query) with no row-cap parameter and no LIMIT injection

recommended_actions: Add a top_k parameter to QuerySQLDatabaseTool and, in _run, either inject a LIMIT top_k clause into the parsed SQL (when absent) or slice the returned rows post-fetch — make the row cap a code-side default, not a prompt instruction.

findings.summary: "Only relevant columns / no SELECT *" rule is prompt-only — no column-narrowing check in code.
findings.description: SQL_PREFIX tells the agent "Never query for all the columns from a specific table, only ask for the relevant columns given the question," but neither QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run nor any layer below it inspects the query's selected columns or rejects SELECT *. The rule is purely a prompt suggestion that an LLM may follow or ignore on any given call.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent MUST query only the columns relevant to the user's question and MUST NOT select all columns of a table indiscriminately.

evidence.snippet: Never query for all the columns from a specific table, only ask for the relevant columns given the question. — prompt-only

recommended_actions: In the parser step recommended for finding PRAX-2026-05-25-001, also reject queries whose SELECT clause is * (or <table>.*) when the table has more than N columns, surfacing a tool error that asks the agent to specify columns explicitly.

findings.summary: No default max_execution_time — per-query timeout depends on operator passing a value.
findings.description: create_sql_agent accepts max_execution_time: Optional[float] = None and forwards it to AgentExecutor, so a runaway query (or a runaway agent loop) has no in-process wall-clock limit unless the caller supplies one. max_iterations=15 does cap the agent's tool-call count, but a single tool-call to run_no_throw on an expensive query can still run for arbitrarily long against the database. The docstring acknowledges this and points operators at server-side controls — the gap is intentional but worth surfacing.

evidence.snippet: max_execution_time: Optional[float] = None — default leaves the loop unbounded in wall-clock terms
docstring lines 96-98: Client-side timeouts do not always guarantee that a running statement is cancelled on the database server.

recommended_actions: Change the create_sql_agent default for max_execution_time to a finite value (e.g., 60 seconds) and document that callers should still set a database-side statement timeout for defense-in-depth.

positives.title: Explicit maintainer security warning in create_sql_agent docstring
positives.description: The create_sql_agent docstring carries a prominent !!! warning block calling out arbitrary-SQL risk, naming least-privilege roles, statement timeouts, and query guardrails as required server-side controls, and pointing operators at Deep Agents for new production work. Honest, specific maintainer disclosure — exactly the shape the RAISE rubric credits.

positives.title: Bounded iteration count enforced in code
positives.description: create_sql_agent defaults max_iterations to 15 and forwards it to AgentExecutor, providing an actual runtime cap on tool-call loops — a real operative control, not a prompt suggestion.

positives.title: Per-cell result truncation reduces injection surface and prompt-context bloat
positives.description: SQLDatabase truncates each result cell to 300 characters via truncate_word, capping the per-row size of attacker-controlled content that can be fed back into the LLM. Does not validate or label content, but is a real width cap.

positives.title: Schema-name sanitization rejects non-identifier characters
positives.description: sanitize_schema in utilities/sql_database.py rejects any schema string not matching ^[a-zA-Z0-9_]+$, preventing the schema argument from carrying an injected fragment when it is interpolated into SET SCHEMA-style statements for hana/duckdb/trino/etc.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files exist within the scoped code paths; the agent emits no structured logs of its own (see finding PRAX-2026-05-25-004).

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc overall. A documented intent is present at the prompt and toolkit-description layer and the framework offers two genuine operative controls (max_iterations default of 15 and a 300-char result-cell truncation in SQLDatabase.run), but every safety-critical remit clause is enforced by the LLM rather than by code: there is no SQL parser, no statement-type allowlist, no row-cap, no scope check, and no log surface on the agent's path. The maintainers' own create_sql_agent docstring frames the design as one that requires server-side controls outside the agent to be safe — which is the honest framing, but it also means the agent in isolation sits in the Absent-to-Ad-hoc band the remit warned against.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The system prompt in agent_toolkits/sql/prompt.py restricts the agent to database Q&A and tells it to return "I don't know" for off-topic questions, and the toolkit hands the agent exactly four named tools — a real domain boundary at the prompt and toolkit-shape layer. Domain enforcement is prompt-only with no code gate, however, so the inference rule "prompt-only domain restriction → cap at 2" applies.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: External live data — query result rows, table names, column names — flows back into the LLM context as plain strings in QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run, InfoSQLDatabaseTool._run, and ListSQLDatabaseTool._run, with no provenance label distinguishing tool output from operator instructions. The 300-character max_string_length cell truncation in SQLDatabase.run reduces the per-cell injection surface but does not validate or label the content.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: The agent's only enforcement of "no DML / no DDL / no admin / no multi-statement / no out-of-scope tables" is the DO NOT make any DML statements line in SQL_PREFIX and the LLM-based sql_db_query_checker — QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run calls self.db.run_no_throw(query) with the model-authored string and no SQL parser, no statement-type allowlist, and no scope check. By the calibration rules ("policy exists + code doesn't implement it" + "prompt-only controls" + "external live data in context without sanitization"), the category lands at Ad hoc rather than Partial.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Mature, actively-maintained library — the create_sql_agent docstring carries an explicit maintainer security warning calling out arbitrary-SQL risk and pointing operators at Deep Agents for new production work, which is exactly the disclosure shape the rubric credits. Dependency pinning and ML-BOM are out of scope for these six files, so the score is conservatively Partial rather than Established.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No adversarial-testing artifacts (red-team reports, injection-test suites, postmortems showing architectural change) are present in the scoped files; the absence may reflect scope (tests live elsewhere in the repo) rather than absence of practice, which is why confidence is Low. The maintainer warning is honest disclosure, not evidence of adversarial-testing-driven design change.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No structured logging calls in base.py, toolkit.py, tool.py, or sql_database.py for any of the agent inputs, tool calls, generated SQL, or returned rows — the only observability surface is verbose=False AgentExecutor output, which is operator-side debug noise rather than a durable action-level record. By the "no logging → cap at 1" inference rule, the category is Ad hoc.
